subject: reading
title: The Origin of Language (TPO 1) - Question 4
content: According to paragraph 3, some scientists argue that language originated when

options.label: A
options.text: Neanderthals evolved into humans

options.label: B
options.text: the physical ability to produce speech appeared

options.label: C
options.text: the first humans needed tools in order to develop language

options.label: D
options.text: Neanderthals discovered a bone that gave them the ability to speak

passage_text:
1 - XPO 1 - The Origin of Language
1  The origin of language is a question that had been widely debated among scientists. When referring to the development of language,
scientists do not just mean the ability to communicate, but more specifically the use of complex language structures, such as syntax
(sentence structure) and grammar. Two camps have emerged to describe how humans first acquired language. On the one side are the
naturalists, who believe there is a genetic and biological predisposition for humans to create language, meaning that the ability to create
complex language is hardwired in the human brain. The opposing side believes, however, that language is a product of environmental
influence. The two hypotheses can also be referred to as "nature" verse "nurture." Because humans or their ancestors began speaking tens of
thousands of years ago, there is little evidence available for modern scientists to observe in order to arrive at a conclusive hypothesis.
2  There have been several different approaches to decide how language was first formed. Researchers have studied apes and other
primates to find the origin of language. Many of these scientists argue that apes have the ability to form language, but do not have the
physiological structure of the mouth and throat to have the capability to actually speak. For example, the bonobo, named Kanzi, is able to
communicate to his trainers through lexigrams—symbols that represent certain words.There is,however, no evidence to sugges that primates
are able to use or understand grammar or syntax.
3  Archaeologists have studied fossils of the first humans and Neanderthals (a subspecies of the first humans) to determine when and how
language developed. Scientists studying the fossilized skeletons of Neanderthals have identified a bone that could have enabled
Neanderthals to speak. This evidence suggests that the ability to produce speech may have been the catalyst for the development of
language. However, other scientists argue that the ability to produce speech is not the same as the ability to use language. They point to
the fact that the bone in question is not present in all Neanderthal skeletons, and that the bone's presence does not necessarily indicate
the ability to speak.
4  Another approach to the study of language is to look at the development of language in children. Scientists have observed that children
are able to learn language without explicit instruction, and that they are able to create new sentences that they have never heard before.
This suggests that there is an innate ability to learn language. However, other scientists argue that this ability is not unique to humans,
and that other animals are also able to learn language through observation and imitation.
5  One of the most famous studies of language development is the case of the Nicaraguan Sign Language (ISN). In the 1970s, a group of
deaf children in Nicaragua were brought together in a school for the deaf. The children had no prior exposure to sign language, and the
teachers at the school were not fluent in sign language. Despite this, the children began to develop their own sign language, which
eventually became a fully developed language with its own grammar and syntax. This case is often cited as evidence for the innate ability
of humans to create language.
6  However, critics of the ISN study argue that the children were not creating language from scratch, but were rather building on the
gestures and signs that they had already developed. They also point out that the children were exposed to some form of communication
through their teachers, who used gestures and signs to communicate with them. This suggests that the development of language may be a
combination of innate ability and environmental influence.
7  In conclusion, the origin of language remains a mystery. While there is evidence to support both the nature and nurture hypotheses,
neither side has been able to provide conclusive proof. The study of language development in children and the case of ISN provide
interesting insights into the process of language acquisition, but more research is needed to fully understand how language first
developed.
analysis: Paragraph 3 states: 'Scientists studying the fossilized skeletons of Neanderthals have identified a bone that could have enabled Neanderthals to speak. This evidence suggests that the ability to produce speech may have been the catalyst for the development of language.' This indicates that some scientists believe language originated when the physical ability to produce speech appeared.

subject: reading
title: The Origin of Language (TPO 1) - Question 5
content: The phrase This evidence in paragraph 3 refers to

options.label: A
options.text: instinctual use of language by the Neanderthals

options.label: B
options.text: the research conducted on primates

options.label: C
options.text: the fossil found by archaeologists

options.label: D
options.text: records discovered by archaeologists

options.label: A
